lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ay, those attires are best: but, gentle nurse,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I pray thee, leave me to myself to-night;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For I have need of many orisons

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To move the heavens to smile upon my state,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which, well thou know’st, is cross and full of sin.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: What, are you busy, ho? need you my help?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: No, madam; we have cull’d such necessaries

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As are behoveful for our state to-morrow:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So please you, let me now be left alone,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And let the nurse this night sit up with you;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For, I am sure, you have your hands full all,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In this so sudden business.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Good night:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Get thee to bed, and rest; for thou hast need.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Farewell! God knows when we shall meet again.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I have a faint cold fear thrills through my veins,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That almost freezes up the heat of life:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I’ll call them back again to comfort me:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Nurse! What should she do here?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My dismal scene I needs must act alone.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come, vial.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What if this mixture do not work at all?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Shall I be married then to-morrow morning?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: No, no: this shall forbid it: lie thou there. Laying down her dagger.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What if it be a poison, which the friar

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Subtly hath minister’d to have me dead,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Lest in this marriage he should be dishonour’d,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Because he married me before to Romeo?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I fear it is: and yet, methinks, it should not,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For he hath still been tried a holy man.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: How if, when I am laid into the tomb,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I wake before the time that Romeo

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come to redeem me? there’s a fearful point!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Shall I not, then, be stifled in the vault,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To whose foul mouth no healthsome air breathes in,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And there die strangled ere my Romeo comes?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or, if I live, is it not very like,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: The horrible conceit of death and night,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Together with the terror of the place—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As in a vault, an ancient receptacle,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where, for these many hundred years, the bones

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Of all my buried ancestors are pack’d:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where bloody Tybalt, yet but green in earth,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Lies festering in his shroud; where, as they say,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: At some hours in the night spirits resort;—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Alack, alack, is it not like that I,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So early waking, what with loathsome smells,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And shrieks like mandrakes’ torn out of the earth,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That living mortals, hearing them, run mad:—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, if I wake, shall I not be distraught,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Environed with all these hideous fears?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And madly play with my forefather’s joints?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And pluck the mangled Tybalt from his shroud?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And, in this rage, with some great kinsman’s bone,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As with a club, dash out my desperate brains?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, look! methinks I see my cousin’s ghost

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Seeking out Romeo, that did spit his body

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Upon a rapier’s point: stay, Tybalt, stay!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Romeo, I come! this do I drink to thee. She falls upon her bed, within the curtains.

glosses.definition: prayers
crossed; contrary
needful; fitting
our ceremonial occasion

glosses.phrase: My dismal scene I needs must act alone
glosses.definition: I must play my grim scene without help — Juliet's theatrical consciousness
cold, weak terror

glosses.phrase: almost freezes up the heat of life
glosses.definition: nearly stops my blood's warmth — the physiology of fear
the Friar's potion
given to me that I might die — Juliet's first suspicion
disgraced — by having married Romeo in secret
Juliet reasons the Friar is too righteous to poison her
suffocated
Juliet imagines suffocation in the sealed vault
the mere thought of death
burial-chamber
container; the tomb as receptacle of the dead
Juliet catalogues the Capulet dead she'll lie among
newly laid in the ground
cries of the dead; folk belief in spirits' wailing
the mandrake root was said to scream when uprooted, killing the hearer

glosses.phrase: environed with all these hideous fears
glosses.definition: surrounded by all these horrors
Tybalt's bone — his body will be nearby, only days old

glosses.phrase: O, look! methinks I see my cousin’s ghost
glosses.definition: Juliet's hallucination just before she drinks
Tybalt's ghost pursuing Romeo — Juliet's imagined revenge-drama
her toast is to Romeo — sacrament of the bridal night

annotations.title: Juliet's potion soliloquy — a tour of her own terror
annotations.body: Left alone with the vial, Juliet's forty-four lines walk through every fear she can imagine and still choose the potion. She asks: What if this is poison — what if the Friar is saving himself from having married me? (She reasons he is too holy.) What if she wakes before Romeo arrives and suffocates in the tomb? What if she wakes among the stinking bones of her ancestors, Tybalt's new corpse fresh? What if ghosts wail and mandrakes scream? What if she goes mad? The whole speech is a study of a young person conducting her own moral audit under maximum pressure — she considers betrayal (by the Friar), suffocation, madness, desecration — and then drinks. Her toast 'Romeo, I come! this do I drink to thee' is both a wedding-night toast and a death-toast, compressed. This is one of Shakespeare's most concentrated studies of a character facing, and exceeding, her own fear.

trials.prompt: Why does Juliet briefly consider the possibility that the Friar's potion is actually poison?
options: She reasons that the Friar might want to be rid of her to conceal his crime of marrying them — and then decides he is too holy for that

trials.prompt: Juliet's toast 'Romeo, I come! this do I drink to thee' compresses —
options: Both a wedding toast (drinking to her groom) and a death toast (drinking the near-poison) — the bridal and funereal registers collapse into one
A prayer to the Virgin Mary

trials.prompt: What specific horror does Juliet imagine about the tomb?
options: Ghosts wailing, mandrakes screaming, Tybalt's fresh corpse nearby, suffocation before Romeo arrives — an accumulating catalogue of specific terrors
That her dress will be ruined
